nodes.name: Specification Section
nodes.desc: A numbered section within a project's technical specifications organized by MasterFormat — defines quality standards, materials, methods, and acceptance criteria for a specific scope of work. Specification sections are the contractual quality standard against which work is inspected and accepted. Each section maps to a MasterFormat number (e.g., 03 30 00 Cast-in-Place Concrete, 09 29 00 Gypsum Board). Sections govern submittals (what must be submitted for approval) and inspections (what must pass).

nodes.name: Standard Cost Code List
nodes.desc: A company-standard or industry-standard template of cost codes used to set up new projects — ensures consistency across an organization's project portfolio. Companies may maintain multiple lists for different project types. Standard lists enable cross-project comparison within a company. The UCDM maps company lists to MasterFormat for cross-company comparison.

nodes.name: WBS Code Structure
nodes.desc: Work Breakdown Structure code templates that provide a second dimension of cost classification beyond cost codes — segments costs by phase, location, category, or other company-defined dimension. WBS codes add granularity within a cost code: cost code 03 30 00 (Concrete) + WBS 'Foundation' vs. WBS 'Elevated Slab'. Key WBS values like 'Rework' and 'Warranty' are signals for rework cost detection. Adoption varies widely.

nodes.name: Budget Code Structure
nodes.desc: Company-specific grouping codes applied to budget line items for executive reporting — distinct from cost codes (transactional) and WBS codes (segmentation). Budget codes group budget lines into roll-up categories (Structure, Enclosure, MEP, General Conditions, Contingency). Less standardized than cost codes but valuable for executive dashboards and portfolio views.

nodes.name: IFC Classification (buildingSMART)
nodes.desc: Industry Foundation Classes — an open data standard for BIM that defines entity types, properties, and relationships for all building elements. IFC entities (IfcWall, IfcDoor, IfcDuctSegment, etc.) provide a machine-readable classification of building elements that complements UniFormat and MasterFormat. Used for BIM coordination, clash detection, and digital twin integration.

nodes.name: UniFormat II
nodes.desc: ASTM-published functional element classification for buildings (E1557). Used in early-design conceptual cost estimating where elements drive cost before specifications exist. 22 main groups (A10 Foundations through G50 Other Site). Largely redundant with MasterFormat for vertical construction execution — kept as meta-reference.

nodes.name: OmniClass
nodes.desc: Holistic 15-table classification spanning the full facility lifecycle. Tables 21 (Elements) and 22 (Work Results) align to UniFormat and MasterFormat respectively. Kept as meta-reference because most tables aren't used day-to-day in construction project management.

nodes.name: ANSI Injury Classification
nodes.desc: The ANSI/BLS system for classifying injuries by nature (laceration, fracture, sprain), body part (hand, back, knee), and cause (fall, struck by, overexertion) — provides detailed injury taxonomy for pattern analysis. More granular than OSHA recordkeeping which only classifies severity. Used for root cause analysis and targeted prevention programs.

nodes.name: International Building Code (IBC)
nodes.desc: The model building code adopted (with local amendments) by most US jurisdictions — governs structural design, fire safety, egress, accessibility, and building system requirements. IBC classification determines construction type (Type I–V), occupancy group (A, B, E, H, I, M, R, S, U), and fire rating requirements. These classifications directly affect project cost, schedule, and building system requirements. Updated on a 3-year cycle.

nodes.name: NFPA Standards
nodes.desc: National Fire Protection Association standards governing fire protection, life safety, and electrical systems — the technical basis for fire code requirements. NFPA 13 (sprinklers), NFPA 72 (fire alarm), NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code), and NEC/NFPA 70 (electrical) are the most impactful on construction. NFPA standards define system design criteria, installation methods, testing requirements, and inspection frequencies.

nodes.name: ADA / Accessibility Standards
nodes.desc: Americans with Disabilities Act Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG) and ICC A117.1 — govern accessible design requirements for public and commercial buildings. Affects door widths, ramp slopes, restroom layouts, signage, elevator requirements, and path of travel clearances. Compliance is both a legal requirement and a project completion condition. Accessibility violations are common punch list items.

nodes.name: Energy Code Standards
nodes.desc: Energy conservation codes (IECC, ASHRAE 90.1) and above-code programs — govern building envelope performance, HVAC efficiency, lighting power density, and renewable energy requirements. Energy code compliance affects construction cost (insulation, glazing performance, equipment efficiency) and requires specific testing and commissioning. Increasingly stringent with each code cycle.

nodes.name: LEED Certification
nodes.desc: Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design — the most widely recognized green building certification system. Point-based system across categories: Sustainable Sites, Water Efficiency, Energy & Atmosphere, Materials & Resources, Indoor Environmental Quality, Innovation. Certification levels: Certified (40–49 pts), Silver (50–59), Gold (60–79), Platinum (80+). LEED requirements affect material selection, construction waste management, commissioning scope, and documentation.

nodes.name: WELL Building Standard
nodes.desc: A performance-based standard focused on occupant health and wellness — covers air, water, nourishment, light, fitness, comfort, and mind. Less common than LEED but growing rapidly, especially in commercial office and healthcare. WELL requirements affect HVAC design (air quality), lighting design (circadian), and finishes (low-VOC materials). Requires ongoing performance testing.

nodes.name: Resilience & Net Zero Standards
nodes.desc: Emerging standards for climate resilience (RELi, FORTIFIED) and net-zero energy/carbon performance — increasingly required by institutional owners and government agencies. Resilience standards address flood, wind, seismic, and wildfire hazards. Net-zero standards require energy production to equal or exceed consumption. Both drive building system design decisions and construction scope.

nodes.name: AIA Contract Documents
nodes.desc: The American Institute of Architects standard contract document series — the most widely used contract forms in US construction. Key documents: A101 (Owner-Contractor, Stipulated Sum), A201 (General Conditions), A401 (Contractor-Sub), G-series (administrative forms: G701 Change Order, G702 Pay Application, G704 Certificate of Substantial Completion). AIA documents define roles, responsibilities, and workflows that directly shape how financial instruments, documents, and quality records flow.

nodes.name: Delivery Method Classification
nodes.desc: The taxonomy of project delivery methods that determines how design, construction, and risk are allocated between parties. Each delivery method creates different document flows, change order patterns, and financial instrument relationships. Key methods: Design-Bid-Build (DBB), CM at Risk (CMAR), Design-Build (DB), Integrated Project Delivery (IPD), Multi-Prime, Construction Management Agency.

nodes.name: AACE Cost Engineering Standards
nodes.desc: The Association for the Advancement of Cost Engineering recommended practices — define cost estimating, scheduling, and project controls methodologies. Key standards: 18R-97 (Cost Estimate Classification), 10S-90 (Cost Engineering), EVMS practices. AACE standards provide the methodology framework for financial metrics (EAC, CPI, SPI) and cost classification (estimate classes 1–5).

nodes.name: Cost Code Standardization Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of a company's projects using a consistent standard cost code list — measures data readiness for cross-project benchmarking. Formula: Projects with Standard Cost Codes / Total Projects × 100. Companies with < 50% standardization have unreliable cross-project financial data. Cost code standardization is the prerequisite for all financial benchmarking.

nodes.name: MasterFormat Mapping Coverage
nodes.desc: Percentage of a company's cost codes that map to recognized MasterFormat divisions — measures the degree to which company codes can be normalized for cross-company comparison. Formula: Cost Codes with Valid MasterFormat Mapping / Total Cost Codes × 100. Some codes (General Conditions, Overhead, Fee) don't map to MasterFormat building systems.

nodes.name: Specification Section Coverage
nodes.desc: Percentage of project specification sections entered as structured data vs. remaining as PDF uploads — measures the quality of specification cross-referencing for submittals and RFIs. Formula: Structured Spec Sections / Estimated Total Sections × 100.

nodes.name: Safety Classification Completeness
nodes.desc: Percentage of safety observations with a structured hazard_name classification — measures the quality of safety data for OSHA hazard analysis. Formula: Observations with hazard_name / Total Safety Observations × 100. Currently 55% populated across the portfolio — a significant enrichment opportunity.

nodes.name: WBS Adoption Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of projects or companies using WBS codes for cost segmentation — measures the depth of financial data granularity available for building-element-level benchmarking. Formula: Projects with WBS Codes / Total Financial Projects × 100.

nodes.name: Certification Adoption Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of projects pursuing third-party certification (LEED, WELL, FORTIFIED, etc.) — measures the market penetration of green building and performance standards. Formula: Certified or Pursuing Projects / Total Projects × 100. Can be segmented by certification type, level, and project type.

nodes.name: Division 00 — Procurement and Contracting Requirements
nodes.desc: Bidding, contracting, and contract documents — the procurement-side of the project file. Includes bid forms, instructions to bidders, contract forms. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 00.

nodes.name: Division 01 — General Requirements
nodes.desc: Project-wide administrative and procedural requirements — submittals, meetings, schedules, quality control, safety, temporary facilities. The 'general conditions' for the work. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 01.

nodes.name: Division 02 — Existing Conditions
nodes.desc: Site investigation, demolition, hazmat abatement, structure relocation. Work performed before new construction starts. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 02.

nodes.name: Division 03 — Concrete
nodes.desc: Cast-in-place, precast, post-tensioned concrete. Includes formwork, reinforcement, mixes, finishes, curing. Foundations, columns, beams, slabs, walls map here. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 03.

nodes.name: Division 04 — Masonry
nodes.desc: Unit masonry (CMU, brick, stone), mortar, anchors, accessories. Veneers, structural masonry walls, chimneys. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 04.

nodes.name: Division 05 — Metals
nodes.desc: Structural steel, light-gauge framing, metal decking, miscellaneous metals, ornamental metals. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 05.

nodes.name: Division 06 — Wood, Plastics, and Composites
nodes.desc: Rough carpentry, finish carpentry, architectural woodwork, plastic and composite fabrications. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 06.

nodes.name: Division 07 — Thermal and Moisture Protection
nodes.desc: Waterproofing, dampproofing, insulation, roofing, siding, fire-stopping, joint sealants. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 07.

nodes.name: Division 08 — Openings
nodes.desc: Doors (wood, hollow metal, aluminum), windows, glazing, hardware, storefronts, curtain walls, skylights. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 08.

nodes.name: Division 09 — Finishes
nodes.desc: Plaster, gypsum board, tiling, terrazzo, acoustical, flooring, wall finishes, paint, wallcoverings. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 09.

nodes.name: Division 10 — Specialties
nodes.desc: Toilet partitions, lockers, fire extinguishers, signage, identifying devices, operable partitions. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 10.

nodes.name: Division 11 — Equipment
nodes.desc: Permanent equipment installed by GC — kitchen, laboratory, athletic, theatrical, medical, library, vault, vending. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 11.

nodes.name: Division 12 — Furnishings
nodes.desc: Window treatments, casework, furnishings, rugs and mats, multiple seating. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 12.

nodes.name: Division 13 — Special Construction
nodes.desc: Pre-engineered structures, swimming pools, ice rinks, kennels, pre-engineered fabric structures. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 13.

nodes.name: Division 14 — Conveying Equipment
nodes.desc: Elevators, escalators, moving walks, dumbwaiters, lifts, hoists, cranes. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 14.

nodes.name: Division 21 — Fire Suppression
nodes.desc: Fire pumps, sprinkler systems, standpipes, fire-extinguishing systems. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 21.

nodes.name: Division 22 — Plumbing
nodes.desc: Plumbing piping, fixtures, equipment, water heaters, drainage. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 22.

nodes.name: Division 23 — HVAC
nodes.desc: Heating, ventilating, air conditioning — boilers, chillers, air handlers, ducts, terminal units, controls. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 23.

nodes.name: Division 25 — Integrated Automation
nodes.desc: Building automation systems — controls, instrumentation, integrated facility management. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 25.

nodes.name: Division 26 — Electrical
nodes.desc: Service entrance, distribution, lighting, branch circuits, motors, generators. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 26.

nodes.name: Division 27 — Communications
nodes.desc: Voice/data, structured cabling, audio-video, distributed antenna, communication systems. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 27.

nodes.name: Division 28 — Electronic Safety and Security
nodes.desc: Access control, video surveillance, intrusion detection, fire alarm, mass notification. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 28.

nodes.name: Division 31 — Earthwork
nodes.desc: Excavation, fill, grading, soil treatments, shoring, dewatering, erosion control. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 31.

nodes.name: Division 32 — Exterior Improvements
nodes.desc: Paving, curbs, walkways, fences, walls, planting, irrigation, site furnishings. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 32.

nodes.name: Division 33 — Utilities
nodes.desc: Water, sanitary sewer, storm drainage, gas, electrical site distribution, communications site. CSI MasterFormat 2024 Division 33.

nodes.name: Subpart C — General Safety and Health Provisions
nodes.desc: Foundation requirements — competent person, training, recordkeeping, accident prevention programs. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart C.

nodes.name: Subpart D — Occupational Health and Environmental Controls
nodes.desc: Medical services, first aid, sanitation, ventilation, illumination, noise. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart D.

nodes.name: Subpart E — Personal Protective and Life Saving Equipment
nodes.desc: PPE for head, eye, foot, hearing, fall protection. Employer responsibility to provide. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart E.

nodes.name: Subpart F — Fire Protection and Prevention
nodes.desc: Firefighting equipment, flammable liquids, hazardous materials storage. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart F.

nodes.name: Subpart G — Signs, Signals, and Barricades
nodes.desc: Traffic control signs, signals, barricades. MUTCD compliance for road work. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart G.

nodes.name: Subpart H — Materials Handling, Storage, Use, and Disposal
nodes.desc: Material storage, rigging, handling. Disposal of waste materials. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart H.

nodes.name: Subpart I — Tools — Hand and Power
nodes.desc: Hand-tool safety, power-tool requirements, abrasive wheels. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart I.

nodes.name: Subpart J — Welding and Cutting
nodes.desc: Gas welding/cutting, arc welding, fire prevention during hot work. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart J.

nodes.name: Subpart K — Electrical
nodes.desc: Electrical installation safety, lockout/tagout, GFCI requirements. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart K.

nodes.name: Subpart L — Scaffolds
nodes.desc: Scaffold design, erection, dismantling. One of the top OSHA citation categories. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L.

nodes.name: Subpart M — Fall Protection
nodes.desc: Duty to provide fall protection at 6 feet; guardrails, safety nets, personal fall arrest systems. The single most-cited OSHA standard year over year. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M.

nodes.name: Subpart N — Cranes, Derricks, Hoists, Elevators, and Conveyors
nodes.desc: Crane operator certification, signal person requirements, inspection. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart N.

nodes.name: Subpart O — Motor Vehicles, Mechanized Equipment, and Marine Operations
nodes.desc: Vehicle safety, equipment operation, marine ops. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart O.

nodes.name: Subpart P — Excavations
nodes.desc: Cave-in protection, trench shoring, sloping/benching, competent person inspection. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P.

nodes.name: Subpart Q — Concrete and Masonry Construction
nodes.desc: Formwork, shoring, post-tensioning, lift-slab construction. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart Q.

nodes.name: Subpart R — Steel Erection
nodes.desc: Bolting, decking, fall protection during steel erection. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart R.

nodes.name: Subpart T — Demolition
nodes.desc: Pre-demolition survey, mechanical demolition, manual demolition, chutes. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart T.

nodes.name: Subpart V — Electric Power Transmission and Distribution
nodes.desc: Line work, substations, energized work practices. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart V.

nodes.name: Subpart W — Rollover Protective Structures; Overhead Protection
nodes.desc: ROPS, FOPS for construction equipment. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart W.

nodes.name: Subpart X — Stairways and Ladders
nodes.desc: Stair rail systems, ladder design, fixed ladders. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart X.

nodes.name: Subpart Z — Toxic and Hazardous Substances
nodes.desc: Lead, asbestos, silica, hazardous chemicals. Cross-references 29 CFR 1910 standards. OSHA construction industry standard 29 CFR 1926 Subpart Z.

nodes.name: Constructability
nodes.desc: Integration of construction knowledge into the planning, design, procurement, and field operations of a project. CII identifies 25+ constructability concepts. Front-end deployment correlates strongly with execution success. Construction Industry Institute (CII) Best Practice.

nodes.name: Front-End Planning
nodes.desc: Comprehensive process for defining the project before authorization. CII research: $1 in front-end planning yields $1.50-$3.00 in execution savings. Single highest-ROI best practice. Construction Industry Institute (CII) Best Practice.

nodes.name: Zero Accident Techniques
nodes.desc: CII-defined safety best practices: pre-task planning, behavioral observation, safety perception surveys, JSA. Mature ZAT correlates with ~50% lower TRIR vs. industry average. Construction Industry Institute (CII) Best Practice.

nodes.name: Advanced Work Packaging
nodes.desc: Integrated planning approach that aligns engineering, procurement, and construction at the work package level. Construction-driven sequence drives engineering and procurement timing. Construction Industry Institute (CII) Best Practice.

nodes.name: Modularization
nodes.desc: Off-site fabrication of project components for site assembly. CII research shows 10-25% schedule compression and improved safety on suitable projects. Construction Industry Institute (CII) Best Practice.

nodes.name: Project Definition Rating Index (PDRI)
nodes.desc: CII scoring framework for scope-definition completeness at the FEL 3 gate. Three published variants — Buildings (64 elements), Industrial Projects (70 elements), and Infrastructure — applied based on project type. Score <200 = well-defined; <500 = acceptable; >500 = at risk. Used at gate reviews to authorize project advancement.
